q: A force of 10 N acts on a body of mass 2 kg initially at rest. The acceleration produced is:
exp: Using Newton’s second law, a = F/m = 10/2 = 5 m/s^2.

q: A passenger in a moving bus leans forward when the bus stops suddenly due to:
exp: The upper body tends to remain in motion due to inertia.

q: The SI unit of impulse is:
exp: Impulse = force × time, so unit is newton-second.

q: Which of the following is true for action and reaction forces?
opts: They act on the same body
exp: According to Newton’s third law, action and reaction act on different bodies.

q: A 5 kg body moving with 4 m/s is brought to rest in 2 s. Retarding force is:
exp: Acceleration = (0−4)/2 = −2 m/s^2. Force = ma = 5×2 = 10 N.

q: When a horse pulls a cart, the cart also pulls the horse backward. Why does the cart move forward?
opts: Ground exerts forward friction on horse
exp: The forward frictional force from the ground moves the system forward.

q: A body of mass m is moving in a circle with constant speed. Which statement is correct?
opts: A centripetal force acts toward center
exp: Direction of velocity changes continuously, requiring centripetal force.

q: The property by which a body resists change in its state of motion is called:
exp: Inertia is the resistance to change in state of rest or motion.

q: A rocket moves upward due to:
exp: Exhaust gases pushed downward produce upward reaction force.

q: A force of 50 N acts on a body for 0.2 s. Impulse produced is:
exp: Impulse = FΔt = 50 × 0.2 = 10 Ns.

q: Assertion (A): Seat belts reduce injuries in accidents. Reason (R): Seat belts increase inertia of passengers.
opts: Both A and R are true and R explains A
Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
exp: Seat belts prevent sudden forward motion due to inertia; they do not increase inertia.

q: A 10 kg block on a frictionless surface experiences acceleration of 3 m/s^2. Net force acting is:
A body moving with constant velocity has:
exp: From Newton’s first law, constant velocity implies zero net force.

q: Which graph represents Newton’s second law correctly?
opts: Force vs acceleration is a straight line through origin
Acceleration vs mass is directly proportional
Momentum vs time is constant always
exp: Since F = ma, force is directly proportional to acceleration.

q: A 2 kg object moving at 6 m/s collides with a wall and rebounds at 4 m/s. Change in momentum is:
exp: Δp = m(v−u) = 2(−4−6) = −20 kg m/s. Magnitude = 20.

q: Why is it difficult to walk on ice?
exp: Walking requires friction between foot and ground.

q: The recoil of a gun is an example of conservation of:
exp: Momentum before and after firing remains conserved.

q: If mass of a body is doubled and same force acts, acceleration becomes:
exp: a = F/m, so acceleration becomes half.

q: A man jumps from a boat onto shore. The boat moves backward due to:
exp: Backward reaction force pushes the boat opposite to jump.

q: The force responsible for changing momentum per unit time is:
exp: Newton’s second law states F = dp/dt.

q: A 4 kg body experiences a net force of 20 N. Its velocity after 5 s if initially at rest is:
A person standing in a lift feels heavier when the lift:
exp: Apparent weight increases when acceleration is upward.

q: The unit of momentum is:
exp: Momentum = mass × velocity.

q: Which of the following is NOT an example of Newton’s third law?
exp: Friction slowing a block mainly involves opposing motion, not action-reaction pair explanation.

q: A force acts on a body but produces no acceleration. The body may be:
exp: Zero acceleration means net force is zero; all are possible.

q: Assertion (A): Mass is a measure of inertia. Reason (R): Larger mass offers greater resistance to acceleration.
opts: Both A and R are true and R explains A
Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
exp: Mass quantifies inertia because larger mass resists acceleration more.

q: A cricket player lowers his hands while catching a ball to:
exp: Increasing collision time reduces force for same impulse.

q: A body moving in a straight line with uniform speed has:
exp: Uniform motion implies net force is zero.

q: A truck and car move with same momentum. Which has greater kinetic energy?
exp: KE = p^2/2m. Smaller mass has greater kinetic energy for same momentum.

q: A force-time graph gives area equal to:
exp: Area under F–t graph equals impulse.

q: A 1 kg body moving at 10 m/s becomes stationary in 0.5 s. Average force is:
Why are passengers advised not to stand in a moving bus?
opts: Due to inertia during sudden stops
exp: Sudden acceleration or braking may topple passengers because of inertia.

q: The net external force on a system is zero. Then:
exp: Zero external force implies conservation of momentum.

q: A body of weight 100 N is on a table. Normal reaction is:
exp: For equilibrium, normal reaction balances weight.

q: A body of mass 3 kg accelerates from 2 m/s to 8 m/s in 3 s. Force applied is:
Which quantity remains constant if no external force acts?
exp: Momentum remains conserved in absence of external force.

q: A balloon filled with air bursts. The air rushes outward due to:
exp: Compressed air escapes due to higher internal pressure.

q: The dimensional formula of impulse is:
exp: Impulse has same dimensions as momentum.

q: A body is acted upon by two equal opposite forces. Then the body:
exp: Zero net force means no acceleration; motion may continue uniformly.

q: A jet plane moves forward because:
exp: Backward expulsion of gases produces forward reaction.

q: A body of mass 2 kg moving at 3 m/s collides elastically and returns at same speed. Impulse magnitude is:
exp: Impulse = change in momentum = 2(−3−3)=−12 Ns. Magnitude 12.

q: Which law explains why dust comes out when a carpet is beaten?
exp: Dust tends to remain at rest due to inertia.

q: The acceleration produced by a force is inversely proportional to:
exp: From F = ma, a ∝ 1/m.

q: A force of 100 N acts on a 20 kg object. Distance covered in 4 s from rest is:
Assertion (A): Every action has an equal and opposite reaction. Reason (R): Action and reaction act simultaneously.
opts: Both A and R are true and R explains A
Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
exp: Both are true, but simultaneity does not explain equality.

q: Which factor does not affect inertia?
exp: Inertia depends mainly on mass, not shape.

q: A person pushes a wall but cannot move it. The wall exerts:
exp: By Newton’s third law, equal and opposite reaction acts.

q: If net force and velocity are perpendicular, then:
exp: Perpendicular force changes direction but not speed.

q: The graph of momentum versus time gives slope equal to:
exp: F = dp/dt, so slope of p–t graph equals force.

q: A block of mass 5 kg rests on a horizontal surface. Coefficient of friction is 0.4. Minimum horizontal force to move it is approximately:
exp: Limiting friction = μmg = 0.4×5×9.8 ≈ 20 N.

q: A body is moving with constant speed in a circular path because:
exp: Centripetal force continuously changes direction of velocity.
